raw_case:
Subcutaneous anterior abdominal wall myositis ossificans
The differential diagnosis of abdominal wall lesions is broad and includes Myositis ossificans (MO). MO primarily arises in skeletal muscles, but can also occur around adjacent structures. It typically appears as a soft tissue lesion with peripheral rim of calcification. The Presented case is of 21-year-old woman with a right lower quadrant mass without a history of trauma but with a positive history of excessive exercise. Radiological images were nonspecific, and tissue biopsy showed features consistent with myositis ossificans. Follow up images revealed findings consistent with the histopathological diagnosis. This case highlights the nonspecific Imaging findings of MO in the early stages and that MO early stage can be prolonged.

Introduction

Myositis ossificans, also known as myositis ossificans traumatica or myositis ossificans circumscripta, is the most common form of Hetertopic ossification [1]. Histopathlogically, MO development has 3 stages. The first stage is defined by the presence of organizing granulation tissue with fibroblastic, osteoblastic tissue differentiation and osteoid formation. The second stage is characterized by the development of mineralized osteoid matrix with immature lamellar bone. These 2 stages last 3-4 weeks consecutively. The third stage is involving the maturation of immature bone into lamellar cortical and trabecular bone, usually occurring after 8 to 10 weeks [1,2]. Imaging features parallel these histopathological stages, ranging from an inflammatory change, enhancing mass, mass with peripheral punctate calcifications to the pathognomonic ossification rim [1]. The case presented in this review, had an atypical time line.

Case presentation

Twenty-one-year-old woman, presented to the clinic with a history of a tender right lower quadrant mass, first noticed 8 months ago. The mass had been slowly increasing in size. The patient reported localized abdominal pain and tenderness aggravated by movement and deep breaths. The pain was not related to her menstrual cycle. She reported a history of excessive exercise but denied any trauma. The patient was healthy without significant past medical or surgical history.Physical examination revealed a tender right lower quadrant subcutaneous firm mass, measuring approximately 5cm, attached to muscles. The overlaying skin was normal without redness or warmth and there was no lymphadenopathy. The general examination was otherwise unremarkable. The biochemical and hematological laboratory results were normal.An initial ultrasound showed a heterogenous lobulated subcutaneous mass, measuring approximately 5.4 × 3 cm. Color Doppler imaging did not reveal internal vascular flow. The mass did not exhibit posterior acoustic shadowing or through enhancement (Fig. 1). The findings were not specific, and further evaluation by cross-sectional imaging was recommended.The patient underwent single-phase contrast-enhanced Computed Tomography (CT), which revealed a right lower quadrant heterogenous subcutaneous soft tissue mass with a few punctate nonperipheral calcifications, abutting the right rectus muscle, without a claw sign. The mass measured 2.5 × 3 cm and was associated with adjacent fast stranding and edema (Fig. 1). No overlaying skin contact or skin thickening was observed. The right inferior epigastric vessels were patent and located medial to the mass. The differential diagnosis after the CT remained nonspecific and broad.To narrow the differential diagnosis, she was referred for a Pelvic Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). The MRI revealed a round soft tissue mass with lobulated margins in the right lower anterior abdominal wall, not directly related to the rectus sheeth. The mass was predominantly of low signal on T1-weighted images (T1WI) and intermediate to high signal on T2WI. No bright signal spots on T1WI to suggest blood product or fat content. The mass measured 3 × 4 × 4.5 cm (AP x TR x CC). The mass demonstrates diffusion restriction and avid postcontrast enhancement (Fig. 2). MRI was negative for ovarian endometriomas, uterine adenomyosis and deep pelvic endometriosis.The constellation of imaging findings were nonspecific, offering a broad differential diagnosis. The differential diagnoses included: desmoid tumor, anterior abdominal wall endometriotic deposit, and Sarcoma. Each diagnosis of the differential has distinct management approaches based on the disease morbidity and mortality. Therefore, tissue diagnosis was required. The patient underwent an Ultrasound Guided biopsy and 5 core biopsies were obtained and sent for histopathology.The histopathology revealed fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation with bone formation/ossification, chronic inflammation and focal fat necrosis (Fig. 3). The tissue was positive for smooth muscle actin (SMA), negative for Desmin, CD34 and ALK-1. These findings suggested heterotopic ossification/myositis ossificans, traumatic or nontraumatic.The patient received Ibuprofen for symptom control and is scheduled for a follow up CT is in 3 months, followed by clinic visit. The Follow-up CT showed a decrease in the size of the mass from 2.5 × 3 cm to 1 × 3 cm with an increase in the size and amount of intralesional calcifications, which were now peripherally located, demonstrating the zone phenomena typically seen in MO (Fig. 4). The follow-up clinic visit, physical examination confirmed the decrease in size and further hardening of the mass, with the patient reporting improvement insymptoms.

Discussion

Myositis ossificans (MO) is a pseudo- inflammatory, benign bone and cartilage forming solitary tumor [3]. It predominantly arises in skeletal muscles, but also occurs around tendons, ligaments, joint capsules, facia and aponeuroses [4] A history of trauma is often present, but not necessary [3]. On pathology, MO demonstrate zone phenomena where ossification forms peripherally and progress in a centripetal pattern [5]. Similarly, imaging of MO typically shows peripheral rim of calcification on X-ray and CT [5]. MRI can demonstrate the zone phenomena as peripheral hyperintensity on the post contrast T1WI of the iso- to mildly hyperintense intramuscular lesion on T1 and T2WI in the acute phase and hypointense on subacute phase indicating mineralization [5]. The distinctive pattern of calcification/ossification is usually not apparent within the first 2 weeks [5].In the case discussed, the mass is located in the subcutaneous tissue of the anterior abdominal wall, at the right lower quadrant, without involvement of the abdominal wall muscles or the rectus sheeth. Despite the relatively long history of the palpable mass (8 months), the initial ultrasound, CT and MRI did not show rim ossification, but revealed few punctate areas of calcification throughout the mass, a distribution atypical for MO. Given the patient's age, duration of symptoms and the lack of typical imaging findings, the differential diagnosis was kept broad, to include desmoid tumor and endometriosis. Since, the imaging features of this case were not typical for any of the other 2 differential diagnosis, as desmoid tumors are typically of low signal intensity on T1 and T2WI MRI images and abdominal wall endometriosis typically demonstrate foci of blood products and small cysts within the deposit, tissue diagnosis was essential. Tissue biopsy revealed findings of early myositis ossificans with predominant fibroblast and myofibroblast with minor component of osteoid formation. The typical radiological features of late MO is evident on follow up CT.

Conclusion

Early myositis ossificans can present a diagnostic dilemma, especially when occurring in an extra-skeletal location. Tissue biopsy plays a major rule in final diagnosis.

Ethics approval and consent to participate

Exempted by King Saud University IRB, IRB Protocol Number: E-24-8549.

Availability of data and materials

Available.

Patient consent

Obtained, however, no patient identifiers included in the Case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 21-year-old woman  
- **Chief Complaint:** Tender right lower quadrant abdominal mass  
- **History of Present Illness:** 8-month history of a slowly enlarging subcutaneous mass with localized pain and tenderness aggravated by movement and deep breaths. No history of trauma but a history of excessive exercise was noted. Pain was not related to the menstrual cycle.  
- **Past Medical History:** None.  
- **Personal History:** History of excessive exercise.  
- **Family History:** None mentioned.  
- **Physical Examination:** Firm, tender, subcutaneous mass (~5 cm) in the right lower quadrant, attached to muscles. Overlying skin was normal without redness or warmth. No lymphadenopathy was present. General examination was unremarkable.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **Ultrasound:** Heterogeneous, lobulated subcutaneous mass (5.4 × 3 cm) with no vascular flow or specific features.  
  - **CT Scan:** Heterogeneous subcutaneous soft tissue mass (2.5 × 3 cm) with adjacent edema and punctate calcifications, but no peripheral ossification.  
  - **MRI:** Lobulated soft tissue mass (3 × 4 × 4.5 cm) with diffusion restriction, intermediate to high T2 signal, and avid post-contrast enhancement. No specific features for alternative diagnoses like endometriosis were found.  
  - **Histopathology (Biopsy):** Fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation with focal ossification, chronic inflammation, and focal fat necrosis. Positive for SMA, negative for Desmin, CD34, and ALK-1.  
  - **Follow-Up CT:** Decrease in size (1 × 3 cm) with increased intralesional, peripheral calcifications consistent with the "zone phenomenon" typical of myositis ossificans.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The initial differential diagnoses included myositis ossificans, desmoid tumor, abdominal wall endometriosis, and sarcoma based on the patient's clinical and imaging findings.  
1. **Myositis Ossificans:**  
   - Considered because of a firm soft tissue mass in a young patient with a history of excessive exercise. However, early imaging findings, such as punctate calcifications (not peripheral rim ossification), did not conform to typical late-stage MO. The follow-up CT demonstrated the peripheral calcification "zone phenomenon," which is pathognomonic for MO, confirming this diagnosis.  
2. **Desmoid Tumor:**  
   - Considered due to the firm mass and attachment to muscle. However, desmoid tumors usually appear as low-signal intensity on T1- and T2-weighted MRI, unlike this case. Also ruled out by histopathology (negative Desmin and CD34).  
3. **Abdominal Wall Endometriosis:**  
   - Considered because of the location and pain. Ruling out findings included the absence of associated blood products or cystic elements on MRI and a lack of menstrual-cycle pain correlation.  
4. **Sarcoma:**  
   - Considered due to nonspecific imaging findings but ruled out by histopathology showing benign fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation without malignancy.  

The definitive diagnosis was achieved through histopathology and supported by follow-up imaging showing evolution consistent with MO.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Subcutaneous anterior abdominal wall myositis ossificans.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Subcutaneous anterior abdominal wall myositis ossificans.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to work through this case. The patient is a 21-year-old woman with a tender right lower quadrant abdominal mass that's been growing slowly over 8 months. The pain gets worse with movement and deep breaths, but there's no history of trauma. She does have a history of excessive exercise. The pain isn't linked to her menstrual cycle, so maybe that rules out something gynecological like endometriosis?

Physical exam shows a firm, tender subcutaneous mass attached to muscles. The skin over it is normal, so no signs of infection or inflammation there. No lymphadenopathy, so probably not a lymph node issue. The imaging is interesting. Ultrasound shows a heterogeneous mass without vascular flow. CT scan mentions edema and punctate calcifications, no peripheral ossification. MRI has diffusion restriction and avid enhancement. Then the follow-up CT shows a decrease in size with increased peripheral calcifications, which they mention is the "zone phenomenon" typical of myositis ossificans. 

The biopsy results show fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation with some ossification, chronic inflammation, and fat necrosis. Immunohistochemistry is positive for SMA (smooth muscle actin), which is seen in myofibroblasts, but negative for Desmin, CD34, and ALK-1. ALK-1 negativity might help rule out some tumors like inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor. CD34 is often positive in other spindle cell tumors but negative here. Desmin negativity suggests it's not a smooth muscle tumor, like leiomyoma or leiomyosarcoma.

Myositis ossificans (MO) comes to mind. MO is a benign lesion that involves heterotopic ossification in muscle or soft tissue, often following trauma. But the patient has no trauma history. Wait, but she does have excessive exercise. Maybe repetitive microtrauma from exercise could lead to MO. The zone phenomenon, which is a zoning pattern of maturation (immature tissue in the center and mature bone at the periphery), is classic for MO. The follow-up CT showing decreased size with peripheral calcifications supports that. 

The other differentials might include soft tissue sarcoma, but the imaging and biopsy results don't seem to support malignancy. The histopathology didn't show high cellularity or atypia. Fibromatosis (desmoid tumor) is another possibility, but those typically show more infiltrative growth and are positive for beta-catenin. The negative CD34 and ALK-1 help exclude some other tumors. Focal fat necrosis might be due to prior injury. 

The history of excessive exercise leading to repetitive strain could be the inciting factor for MO, even without overt trauma. The location in the abdominal wall muscles, the imaging findings, and the histopathology with ossification and fibroblastic proliferation all fit MO. The zone phenomenon on follow-up CT is a key clue here. So putting it all together, the diagnosis is likely myositis ossificans.
results.content:
```
### Reasoning:
<step 1> The patient’s clinical presentation includes a tender, subcutaneous mass in the right lower quadrant attached to muscles, with pain exacerbated by movement and deep breathing. A history of excessive exercise (a potential source of repetitive microtrauma) and no association with menstrual cycles or trauma suggests a musculoskeletal or reactive process rather than a gynecological etiology.

<step 2> Imaging findings are critical. Ultrasound and CT show a heterogeneous mass with calcifications, while MRI reveals diffusion restriction and avid enhancement. The follow-up CT demonstrates a reduction in size and peripheral calcifications forming a "zone phenomenon," a hallmark of myositis ossificans (MO). This pattern reflects maturation from central cellular tissue to peripheral ossification.

<step 3> Histopathology confirms fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation with focal ossification and fat necrosis. Immunohistochemistry (SMA+, Desmin-, CD34-, ALK-1-) supports a myofibroblastic lesion without features of desmoid tumors (beta-catenin negative not mentioned) or inflammatory myofibroblastic tumors (ALK-1 negative). The absence of malignancy markers and the presence of ossification align with MO.

<step 4> The absence of trauma history is addressed by considering repetitive microtrauma from excessive exercise as the inciting factor. MO can develop without overt trauma, and the clinical, imaging, and histopathological features collectively confirm this diagnosis over alternatives like sarcoma or endometriosis.

### Answer:
Myositis ossificans

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s clinical presentation includes a tender, subcutaneous mass in the right lower quadrant attached to muscles, with pain exacerbated by movement and deep breathing. A history of excessive exercise (a potential source of repetitive microtrauma) and no association with menstrual cycles or trauma suggests a musculoskeletal or reactive process rather than a gynecological etiology.

Imaging findings are critical. Ultrasound and CT show a heterogeneous mass with calcifications, while MRI reveals diffusion restriction and avid enhancement. The follow-up CT demonstrates a reduction in size and peripheral calcifications forming a "zone phenomenon," a hallmark of myositis ossificans (MO). This pattern reflects maturation from central cellular tissue to peripheral ossification.

Histopathology confirms fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation with focal ossification and fat necrosis. Immunohistochemistry (SMA+, Desmin-, CD34-, ALK-1-) supports a myofibroblastic lesion without features of desmoid tumors (beta-catenin negative not mentioned) or inflammatory myofibroblastic tumors (ALK-1 negative). The absence of malignancy markers and the presence of ossification align with MO.

The absence of trauma history is addressed by considering repetitive microtrauma from excessive exercise as the inciting factor. MO can develop without overt trauma, and the clinical, imaging, and histopathological features collectively confirm this diagnosis over alternatives like sarcoma or endometriosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: The initial differential diagnoses included myositis ossificans, desmoid tumor, abdominal wall endometriosis, and sarcoma based on the patient's clinical and imaging findings.

Myositis ossificans was considered due to a firm soft tissue mass in a young patient with a history of excessive exercise; early imaging showed punctate calcifications, which did not conform to typical late-stage MO, but follow-up CT demonstrated the peripheral calcification "zone phenomenon," confirming this diagnosis.

Desmoid tumor was considered due to the firm mass and attachment to muscle; however, desmoid tumors typically show low-signal intensity on T1- and T2-weighted MRI, unlike this case, and were ruled out by negative Desmin and CD34 histopathology.

Abdominal wall endometriosis was considered due to the location and pain; it was ruled out by the absence of associated blood products or cystic elements on MRI and the lack of menstrual-cycle pain correlation.
Sarcoma was considered due to nonspecific imaging findings but was ruled out by histopathology showing benign fibroblastic/myofibroblastic proliferation without malignancy.
The definitive diagnosis was achieved through histopathology and supported by follow-up imaging showing evolution consistent with MO.
Final diagnosis: Subcutaneous anterior abdominal wall myositis ossificans.
